speaker: interviewer
text: --number four. This is Magda Weiss. How long were you in the hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Around three weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you, at that time-- had-- had you known for sure what had happened to everyone in your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Still, I did not know, though I did hear this-- my brother survive, because it cannot be somebody else. And daily we got the list of survivors. But we was also, by this time, told what we should expect for our parents and the young childrens.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do when you left the hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: I just went back to the camp. And we have to make a decision what would happen. In this time, the Russian was in Hungary. It was very dangerous to go back to Hungary, because you have to take a chance by foot, or sometime by train. And though many people did take their chance, we was advised not to do it. But still, those who has family there and left their family hided-- took this chance. But they was told, with the Russian there is quite a danger to met, especially for the woman folks. Because sometime they was quite rough over on the woman, which was very true. I met quite few which said thing happened because they didn't care what. And they was different type of a soldiers than the English or the American.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any dealings with the Russians?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what decision did you make?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I decided I never want to go back to Hungary after what happened. The whole family was wiped out. But I was high hope-- this, for one of my sister might still in life, the one who was hiding. I have this feeling she might be in life-- the only one in Hungary. And I decided, if I could make a chance for them to take them out, if I have a way to go to Israel, I would like to go to Israel. This was my decision.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- waited for-- I registered myself. [LAUGHS] And finally, I tried to register myself to the kibbutz, hoping it would be faster to get a transfer to Israel. But it just did not happen. It was delayed and delayed again and again.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the one in our kibbutz who was gone to Israel, was brought back, because they was the one who got this boat. What was the name of the boat? Remember? Mike know the name of the boat who was brought back to Germany. What were you doing in the meantime after you left the hospital? Did you go straight to the kibbutz?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not right, just lived in Bergen-Belsen and do nothing, really. Just this was a time I was hardly working-- just live day by day and talking about-- hoping what else we can do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the kibbutz. What was the name of the kibbutz?

speaker: interviewee
text: The kibbutz name, the one I was, the Hashomer Hatzair.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Eschwege. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- when did you go to the kibbutz?

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I stayed there till the-- '47 even until '40-- beginning of the '48, just hoping to land-- [LAUGHS] --and land, and then, finally, we did realize that this will never happen, not with this kibbutz. So the people, many of them left the kibbutz and gone back to the camps, and hoping to leave Germany as soon as possible.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, again, I registered myself to United State and to Canada. And then, finally, the closest chance I got is to-- signed one year domestic work with Canada. And then there was with me about another 30 girls who did the same. And we was all allowed to emigrate to Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: From the time that you left the kibbutz till the time you immigrated to Canada, were you working at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was working in-- again, in a hospital, where-- [LAUGHS] --children was born. You know, a lot of women get pregnant, and children was born in Germany. So I was working, again, just as a helper for the midwife, who was nurse. And I was guarding the children, feeding them, changing their diapers. And I was good in it. [LAUGHS] I was told I was good in it-- and then bringing the babies to the mother when they have to nurse it. One day, I made a mistake. One girl has-- one mother has a girl. I give her a boy. [LAUGHTER] And I was told you made a mistake. But they said, that's OK. Because I know hers was a boy, mine was a girl, so exchange and-- but it was a lot of-- time was passing. You know, meantime, I also working in registry, in the office. Many people come this time from Russia and-- with their whole family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you alone still at that point, or had you met any friends? Had you developed any relationships at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. I was alone. But I have a lot of friend. At this time, my-- know my husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had met your husband already there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you meet your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the kibbutz.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the kibbutz.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the kibbutz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Michael-- Michael Weiss. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. When did you-- did you come to Canada together?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. I have a chance to come first.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you come to Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: '48, September.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you live? Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: We live in Sarnia.

speaker: interviewer
text: As a domestic?

speaker: interviewee
text: As a domestic, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you treated?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was treated fine. I was spending with a Jewish-- first I was with a Christian family. But I ask for a change, because I wanted to live with a Jewish family. And they gave me this chance, after six weeks. The people was very, very mad at me, the one whom I left. I have two nice childrens. And-- but still, there was three girls in Sarnia, both of my friends. The other two was with a Jewish family, and my heart was just belong there. And after six weeks, I changed my work.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you treated by the Jewish family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like a daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Zealers.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do there? Did they have children? Did you work with the children?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, they have just one grown-up boy. His name was David Davies [NON-ENGLISH], about 20 years old. And his brother was a pilot lost in the war somewhere over Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you talk about your experiences with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] No, not as much-- not as much, because they thought I was very sad. And they want me to laugh and be happy. They really take me to movies. They want to feed me like they thought I could eat in the world, but I couldn't. And they just want to forgot what I have lived through, because they know. But I wasn't able. So they really tried to change me over to become a Canadian person. And they did their best.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed with them almost the end of the year. But after this, I was transferred to Toronto, to Toronto General Hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do in the hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a nursing aide.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did your husband came to Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: He come two years later, 1950, the beginning of 1950.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you married in Europe, or were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, we wasn't married in Europe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your wedding.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my wedding was a-- a lovely wedding, in a beautiful Canadian home, the home of Mrs. Goode. I was allowed to have as many friend as I wished, which was, this time, about 50. She covered all the expense, and treated us like a daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you meet Mrs. Goode?

speaker: interviewee
text: Through the friend of the Zealers.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did your husband come to Canada? Did he have someone sponsor him?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was Mrs. Goode who sponsored him. She got this time a hotel and a restaurant. She wasn't working in it, but she was the one who own it. So she signed for him to work, and room and board, whenever he came. Though Mike did not take advantage of this. He was looking work of his own.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you work when-- when you came to Toronto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, I always work.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work were you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, after I had my year was over, I still work for another year in a hospital. And after I was working to Dominion Regalia and [NON-ENGLISH], and Regalia doing the embroidery work, which I do know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which your grandmother taught you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, oh, yes. And I was the first immigrant in Dominion Regalia. Never was there an immigrant before. But very few people know the trade. And when he interviewed me, he never interview a Jew before.

speaker: interviewee
text: When he asked me my religion, I told him I'm Jewish. And he said, what's this? I said, this is a religion. Don't you ever heard of a Jewish religion?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I know by this time, I would never employed by him. He said, no.

speaker: interviewee
text: I asked him this time--

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: --very ignorantly, did you ever heard Jesus? He was a Jew. And I told him right in his face. And he did employ me. But he said, don't tell anybody this-- you are a Jew. Why do you think he told you that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because he has a factory who could never employ neither a Jew, neither a Catholic. We was working to the Grand Lodge. This is the orange lodge. And if they would know it, it would harm his business.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of embroidery were you doing there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was making the sample work. Whenever he get an order, he get a picture of it, he has to make a few sample. You have to do this. Say, you might get yourself-- and you get a picture of this person. And you have to do your best. You have to design it, make a face, whatever it is. And that's the type of work. You're working with different gold, silver, many different way. And he usually get the order. As a matter of fact, one day, he said I got 3,000 order after this sample you made. It was the drummer boy. I should have kept a few, because he said, you take all the time you ever need. If it's a week, a week. If it's months, it takes you months. It doesn't matter. But I want to get the order. And I was very lucky on the way, because I know how to design. I know how to paint faces and things. And he treated us-- well paid. And if we overwork, he gave us supper, the best of supper-- whatever you order. So he was a very good boss. And I made good money.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you work in any other jobs other than the embroidery here in Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do when you finished?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I finished the embroidery, I-- my eyes was kind of hurt. And we have a bit of money. And we decide to buy a house, which we have $2,000 this time, and we bought a house for a down payment. It was a little house, but we was very happy in it. It was our first home.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your husband do for a living here in Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mike was working in a-- clothing factories. He was working as a-- ironing very, very hard work, because in the summer, it's very hot. And he also was working some other places. But finally, he end up in a hospital as an orderly, in Toronto, Sunnybrook, because it was an easier work there-- steady, and better paid.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you talk about your war experiences with people here in Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: About my war experiences? No-- only by Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you want to talk about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I would.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why didn't you talk to other people?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, they don't believe-- I have the feeling they wouldn't believe me. Even the Jews sometime don't believe you. How could you stay in life? I even know my girlfriend, who has a survivor as a father, and his own son hardly believes his story-- who-- is a Polish man. And most of the gentile people in the hospital where I was working, I was hearing them to talking about the Jews. And they talk about this-- all these Jews who was in camp-- it's hardly true, but they get big money now for Germany and other things like this. And only the European know it is true-- none the Canadian. They just-- the only one who known is the soldiers who was there and met with the people in the camp who was freed them. And have their own experience. The other one till can't. These are stories.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Weiss, why did you come forward to tell your story?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: As I'm talking between other friends who was in the concentration camp, and we like to find out if God create us-- us, we are the people of God, number one, the Bible and everything, why all this is-- did happen with us? And we kind of like to have some answer, at least some answer.

speaker: interviewee
text: And maybe it's our own fault to find out something good which could come out of this. And the only one we really find out-- maybe it was the price of Israel. This-- the reason us of telling this story. And other true reason is I want to have a memory of my people who died for this, or for whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any children, Mrs. Weiss?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you had a message you could leave the world, what would you tell them?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] You might-- don't like it. I want only one God over the whole world. It has to be named God, just God. It should not have, really, 100 or 1,000 religion, because if all the blood was flown for over religion, all the war has a lot to do with religion. And therefore, I think, if religion wouldn't be one million type, we have to accept everyone of us, that God is inside of us, and God created us to loving and imagine-- image in him. And I agree with Moses-- [LAUGHS] --the one who said, if there is this God, it would be a God for every people in the world.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much, Mrs. Weiss.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this what my belief. This is the picture of me. It was taken in Hungary. I am 17 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] What year was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in 1943. In-- This picture is myself. It has been taken after the war, in a German hospital, at 1945, in June. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This picture is in the kibbutz in Eschwege-- my future husband on the third on the left, at 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: In which row is he?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the top row. This picture taken in Toronto, in the Toronto General Hospital. I am the fourth in the middle.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Where? In the top row? In the bottom?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the bottom row. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This picture is my oldest sister, taken in Budapest, at 1950, after she lost her husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is Teresa.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's her last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her last name is Rady, R-A-D-Y. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is a legitimation card, which was given to the people who was in concentration camp. And Americans freed us. And it has been stamped and witnessed-- also, in the back-- to use this as our paper, because we lost all our other papers.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you get this, Mrs. Weiss?

speaker: interviewee
text: We get this at 1945. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is our wedding picture, 1940-- 1950, the 30 of July, Saturday, after sundown. The lady on the left is Mrs. Goode, who took over my husband from Germany. She arranged all for our wedding. And she also did this for another 8 or 10 girls who lost their families.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who else is on that picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the rabbi, I think, the rabbi, and Mrs. Goode, and some of our guests.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where are you and your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, myself is in the wedding dress; my husband next to me. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name is Michael. And on the left is Mrs. Goode, who is my mother this time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a work. It called metallic embroidery, which my grandmother teached us to do. It was her specialty in Hungary as an embroidery teacher.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who made this piece, Mrs. Weiss?

speaker: interviewee
text: I made this piece myself at the factory. I used to work in Canada, after I came to Canada. The factory name was Dominion Regalia, at Elm Street. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this is a hobby of my. I am painting, China painting, with oil.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my family, my husband on my right, Miklos, my niece on my back, Susan Wollman and my nephew, Ben Wollman.
